metadata.title: Domain-Driven Design Principles
metadata.source: Domain-Driven Design: Tackling Complexity in the Heart of Software by Eric Evans
metadata.description: Atomic principles extracted from the DDD book for use in code refactoring and design decisions

principles.name: Ubiquitous Language
principles.definition: A common, rigorous language shared between developers and domain experts, used in all communication and directly reflected in the code. The vocabulary includes names of classes, operations, and domain concepts.
keyCharacteristics: Used consistently in all team communication (spoken, written, diagrams)
Directly reflected in code (class names, method names, module names)
Based on the domain model, not technical jargon
Evolves as the model evolves
Makes implicit concepts explicit
Eliminates ambiguity between business and technical terms
whenToApply: From the very beginning of a project
When domain experts and developers use different terms for the same concept
When technical terms obscure business meaning
When team members have different interpretations of the same term
During knowledge crunching sessions with domain experts
codeSmells: Business logic hidden in procedures with technical names
Developers translating between business terms and code terms
Domain experts don't understand conversations about the code
Multiple terms for the same concept in different parts of the system
Comments needed to explain what code represents in business terms
Method names like 'doProcess()' or 'handleData()' instead of domain-specific verbs
refactoringGuidance: Rename classes, methods, and modules to match domain terminology
Extract implicit concepts into explicit model elements
Work with domain experts to refine awkward or ambiguous terms
Document the language in a glossary that evolves with the model
Iron out difficulties by experimenting with alternative expressions
Recognize that a change in language is a change to the model
Make language usage pervasive - use it everywhere until it flows naturally

examples.bad: class Booking { void deleteRows() { /* removes shipment data */ } }
examples.good: class Cargo { void clearRoute() { /* removes routing plan when specs change */ } }

principles.name: Bounded Context
principles.definition: An explicit boundary within which a domain model applies. Within the boundary, all terms and concepts have specific meaning and the model is kept strictly consistent. Different models apply in different contexts.
keyCharacteristics: Defines clear scope where a model is applicable and consistent
Aligned with team organization and ownership
Has physical manifestations (code base, database schema)
Contains its own Ubiquitous Language
Models can differ across context boundaries
Integration across boundaries requires explicit translation
whenToApply: When multiple models exist in a large system
When different teams work on different parts of the system
When integrating with external systems
When different parts of the domain have different consistency requirements
To prevent model fragmentation and confusion
When the same term means different things in different parts of the domain
codeSmells: Code fragmentation where similar concepts exist in multiple places
False cognates - same term with different meanings causing confusion
Duplicate concepts that need to be synchronized
Unclear where one model ends and another begins
Teams stepping on each other's code
Database contradictions from conflicting models
Integration code scattered throughout the system
refactoringGuidance: Explicitly map out existing contexts in the system
Define boundaries in terms of team organization and code ownership
Align physical code structure (modules, packages) with context boundaries
Create explicit translation layers at context boundaries
Document context boundaries and their relationships in a Context Map
Keep each context's model internally consistent
Don't share code across contexts without explicit integration strategy
Recognize that contexts are not the same as modules

contexts: Shopping Context: 'Product' focuses on catalog info, pricing, availability
Fulfillment Context: 'Product' focuses on physical dimensions, weight, shipping constraints
Accounting Context: 'Product' focuses on cost, revenue, tax categories

principles.name: Entity
principles.definition: An object defined primarily by its identity rather than its attributes. Entities have continuity through a life cycle and must be distinguished from other objects even when attributes are similar.
keyCharacteristics: Has a unique identity that persists through time and state changes
Identity is fundamental, attributes are secondary
Can change form and content radically while maintaining continuity
Distinguished from other objects even with identical attributes
Thread of continuity must be maintained throughout lifecycle
Identity may be generated internally or come from external source
whenToApply: When objects need to be tracked across different states
When identity matters independent of attribute values
When objects have a lifecycle with significant state changes
When objects need to be matched or retrieved by identity
When mistaken identity would lead to data corruption
Examples: User accounts, orders, transactions, customers
codeSmells: Using object equality (==) for domain identity checks
No clear identity field or operation
Identity based on mutable attributes
Multiple objects representing the same entity without clear identity
Identity generation not guaranteed to be unique
Complex entities with too many attributes obscuring identity
Value objects being tracked by identity unnecessarily
refactoringGuidance: Define what it means to be 'the same thing' for each entity
Implement explicit identity operation (equals/hashCode based on ID)
Keep entity definition focused on identity and lifecycle
Strip entity down to intrinsic characteristics that define identity
Move behavior and attributes to associated objects when appropriate
Design means of distinguishing each object regardless of form or history
Ensure identity remains immutable once established
Use generated IDs when no natural key exists
For entities with natural keys, validate uniqueness constraints

examples.rationale: Two deposits of same amount on same day are still distinct transactions

examples.attributes: Name, address, phone used for matching but don't define identity

principles.name: Value Object
principles.definition: An object that describes some characteristic of a thing and has no conceptual identity. Value Objects are defined by their attributes and are interchangeable when attributes match.
keyCharacteristics: Defined by attributes, not identity
Immutable - cannot change state after creation
Interchangeable - any instance with same attributes is equivalent
Can be shared safely across entities
Should express a conceptual whole (Whole Value pattern)
Can contain other value objects or reference entities
Often used as attributes of entities
whenToApply: When you only care about attributes, not which instance you have
To describe characteristics or measurements
When objects represent descriptive aspects of the domain
For concepts like money, date ranges, addresses, colors
When immutability and shareability are beneficial
To reduce complexity of entities by extracting attributes
codeSmells: Value objects with mutable state
Value objects with identity fields or tracking
Primitive obsession - using primitives instead of value objects
Scattered attributes that should form a conceptual whole
Bidirectional associations between value objects
Value objects holding references meant to be tracked
Large entities with many primitive attributes that could be value objects
refactoringGuidance: Make value objects immutable
Implement value equality based on all attributes
Group related attributes into whole value objects
Consider pass-by-value semantics (copy when needed)
Eliminate bidirectional associations between value objects
Extract value objects from bloated entities
Design for safe sharing and copying
Use value objects to encapsulate complex calculations
Make value objects side-effect-free

examples.note: Identity depends on context - Value Object in e-commerce, Entity in postal service

principles.name: Aggregate
principles.definition: A cluster of associated objects treated as a unit for data changes. Each aggregate has a root entity and a boundary. The root is the only member that external objects can hold references to.
keyCharacteristics: Defines a consistency boundary
Has one root entity that controls access
Root has global identity, internals have local identity
Invariants enforced within aggregate boundaries
External objects can only reference the root
Transient references to internals allowed during operations
All changes go through the root
whenToApply: To define transaction boundaries for consistency
When groups of objects must change together to maintain invariants
To manage complexity of object relationships
To prevent unauthorized access to internal objects
When defining locking scope for concurrent access
To clarify ownership relationships in the model
codeSmells: Direct external references to internal aggregate objects
Invariants that span multiple aggregates
Unclear transaction boundaries
Aggregates that are too large or too small
No clear root entity
External objects modifying internal aggregate state directly
Cascading updates across multiple aggregates
Concurrent access issues from improper locking scope
refactoringGuidance: Identify natural consistency boundaries in the domain
Choose one entity as the aggregate root
Enforce that external access only goes through the root
Make root responsible for checking all aggregate invariants
Root should provide methods that maintain invariants
Delete operation should remove entire aggregate
Only root should be retrievable via repository queries
Consider splitting aggregates that are too large
Design aggregates based on consistency requirements, not just associations
Allow only roots to hold references to other aggregate roots

invariants: Each wheel position has exactly one tire
Total tire mileage equals sum of position mileages
Sum of line item amounts <= PO approved limit
examples.note: Concurrent edits to different line items need aggregate-level locking

principles.name: Repository
principles.definition: Provides the illusion of an in-memory collection of all objects of a certain type. Encapsulates database access and reconstitution of objects, presenting a simple interface for retrieving aggregate roots.
keyCharacteristics: Acts like a collection with querying capability
Only created for aggregate roots that need global access
Encapsulates storage and query technology
Provides methods to add, remove, and find objects
Returns fully instantiated objects
Client talks in terms of the model, not database
Hides complex data retrieval machinery
whenToApply: For aggregate roots that need to be retrieved by clients
To decouple domain model from persistence technology
When you need flexible querying of domain objects
To centralize data access logic
When you want to substitute implementations (e.g., for testing)
To communicate design decisions about object access
codeSmells: Data access code scattered throughout domain objects
Domain objects depending on persistence framework
Complex SQL queries in business logic
No clear way to retrieve objects
Direct database access from application services
Repositories for non-root entities
Repository methods returning partially initialized objects
Repositories that expose database concepts to clients
refactoringGuidance: Create repositories only for aggregate roots
Design interface in terms of the domain model, not database
Encapsulate all data access behind repository interface
Provide methods that select objects based on domain criteria
Return fully reconstituted aggregates
Consider specification pattern for complex queries
Separate query methods from command methods where appropriate
Use repositories to communicate access patterns in the design
Keep implementation details hidden from clients
Provide hard-coded query methods for common cases

principles.name: Factory
principles.definition: Encapsulates the knowledge and process of creating complex objects and aggregates. Provides an interface that reflects client goals and abstracts the construction details.
keyCharacteristics: Responsibility is creating other objects
Hides construction complexity
Creates entire aggregates as a piece
Enforces all invariants of created objects
Each creation method is atomic
Abstracted to type desired, not concrete class
May be a factory method, abstract factory, or standalone factory
whenToApply: When object creation is complex
When creation logic doesn't belong in the object being created
To enforce aggregate invariants during creation
When you want to hide concrete classes
When creation involves assembling multiple related objects
To centralize creation rules and policies
codeSmells: Complex constructors with many parameters
Object creation logic scattered throughout code
Objects created in invalid states
Client code depends on concrete classes
Duplicate creation logic
Creation logic mixed with business logic
Invariants not enforced at creation time
Complicated object assembly in client code
refactoringGuidance: Extract complex construction into factory method or class
Make factory atomic - produce valid object or fail clearly
Enforce all invariants in factory
Place factory close to objects with natural relationship to product
Use factory method on aggregate root for creating internal entities
Create standalone factory when no natural host exists
Factory should produce entire aggregate, not just root
Return abstract interface, hide concrete implementation
Consider factory for reconstitution from database (work with repository)

examples.example: PurchaseOrder.newLineItem(Part, quantity) - hides internal LineItem creation
BrokerageAccount.createTradeOrder(security, shares) - embeds account info and enforces trading rules
LoanFactory.createLoan(terms, applicant) - complex validation and assembly

principles.name: Service
principles.definition: An operation that represents a domain concept that doesn't naturally belong to an entity or value object. Services are stateless operations defined in terms of the domain model.
keyCharacteristics: Represents domain operation, not a thing
Stateless - no state affects behavior
Interface defined in terms of domain model elements
Named for activity (verb), not entity (noun)
Can exist in domain, application, or infrastructure layers
Parameters and results are domain objects
Part of ubiquitous language
whenToApply: When operation doesn't naturally belong to an entity or value object
For significant domain processes or transformations
When operation involves multiple domain objects
For calculations that don't fit one object
When forcing into an entity would distort its meaning
Domain Services: transfer funds, calculate route, authenticate user
codeSmells: Entities or value objects with operations that don't fit their concept
Anemic domain model - all logic in services
Services that should be entity behavior
Manager/Handler/Processor classes with no clear domain meaning
Stateful services holding domain state
Services in wrong layer (domain vs. application vs. infrastructure)
Too many services stripping behavior from entities
refactoringGuidance: First try to place behavior in entity or value object
Create service only when it truly doesn't belong to an object
Define interface using ubiquitous language
Keep service stateless
Distinguish domain services from application services
Domain service: significant domain concept, contains business logic
Application service: orchestrates domain objects, no business logic
Name services after activities in the domain
Three characteristics: relates to domain concept, interface in terms of model, stateless

examples.note: Involves two accounts, represents banking domain concept
Complex calculation not natural for any one entity

principles.name: Module
principles.definition: Organizes related domain concepts into cohesive units. Module names become part of the ubiquitous language and should reflect domain insight, not technical partitioning.
keyCharacteristics: Communicates conceptual organization
Names reflect domain concepts
Low coupling between modules
High cohesion within modules
Should coevolve with the model
Tell the story of the system
Part of ubiquitous language
whenToApply: To organize complex domains into understandable chunks
When concepts naturally group together
To limit cognitive load by chunking related concepts
To communicate design structure
To enforce architectural boundaries
codeSmells: Modules named after technical layers (controllers, services, models)
Modules not reflecting domain concepts
High coupling between modules
Low cohesion within modules
Module structure never refactored
Module names not used in conversations
Framework-driven packaging obscuring domain structure
refactoringGuidance: Name modules after domain concepts, not technical patterns
Choose modules that tell story of the system
Seek conceptually cohesive groups of objects
Minimize coupling between modules
Refactor modules as understanding deepens
Use module names in conversation with domain experts
Modules are chapters in the domain story
Avoid infrastructure-driven packaging schemes
Keep domain objects together even if technically separated

principles.name: Layered Architecture
principles.definition: Separates concerns into layers: User Interface, Application, Domain, and Infrastructure. The Domain layer contains the business logic and is isolated from technical concerns.
keyCharacteristics: Domain layer isolated from infrastructure and UI
Each layer depends only on layers below
Domain layer is the heart of the software
Infrastructure provides technical capabilities
Application layer coordinates domain objects
UI layer presents information and accepts input
whenToApply: In all domain-driven designs
To separate business logic from technical concerns
To make domain model clear and testable
When domain complexity justifies the investment
codeSmells: Business logic in UI or infrastructure code
Domain objects depending on frameworks
Database concerns mixed with business rules
Application logic in domain objects
No clear separation of concerns
Can't test business logic without full stack
refactoringGuidance: Extract domain logic from UI and infrastructure
Create clear interfaces between layers
Make domain layer framework-independent
Move coordination logic to application layer
Keep domain focused on business rules
Use dependency inversion for infrastructure dependencies

principles.name: Anti-Corruption Layer
principles.definition: An isolating layer that translates between your domain model and external systems or legacy code. Prevents the external model from corrupting your clean model.
keyCharacteristics: Translates between different models
Protects domain model from external influence
Reabstracts external concepts in your domain terms
One-way dependency (your model doesn't depend on external)
May contain adapters, facades, and translators
Exposes only features your application needs
whenToApply: When integrating with legacy systems
When external system has incompatible model
To prevent external concepts from invading your model
When you can't change the external system
To maintain model integrity at boundaries
codeSmells: External system concepts leaking into your model
Direct dependencies on external system interfaces
Your model distorted to accommodate external system
Translation logic scattered throughout code
Confusion between your concepts and external concepts
refactoringGuidance: Create explicit layer between your model and external system
Translate external concepts to your domain language
Facade external system to expose only needed features
Make translation explicit and centralized
Keep external model from influencing your design decisions

examples.approach: Create AllocationChecker class that translates sales data into domain concepts like EnterpriseSegment

principles.name: Shared Kernel
principles.definition: A subset of the domain model shared between two bounded contexts. Both teams agree not to change it without consultation and coordinate continuous integration of the shared parts.
keyCharacteristics: Explicitly shared between teams
Requires coordination to change
Usually small, carefully chosen subset
Reduces duplication across contexts
Both teams must agree on changes
Often includes core domain or generic subdomains
whenToApply: When two teams have significant overlap in models
When teams can coordinate closely
To reduce translation burden between related contexts
When core domain is shared across teams
When cost of coordination is less than cost of duplication
codeSmells: Shared code changed without coordination
Shared kernel too large
No clear agreement on what is shared
Teams avoiding changes because of sharing overhead
Confusion about ownership of shared code
refactoringGuidance: Explicitly define what is in shared kernel
Keep shared kernel small
Establish process for coordinating changes
Both teams test against shared kernel
Consider splitting if coordination becomes too difficult
Document shared kernel boundaries clearly

principles.name: Customer-Supplier
principles.definition: Two teams in upstream-downstream relationship where downstream depends on upstream. Establish clear customer-supplier relationship with automated acceptance tests for the interface.
keyCharacteristics: Clear upstream (supplier) and downstream (customer) relationship
Downstream team's needs drive upstream planning
Automated tests define interface contract
Formal planning and negotiation process
Works best when teams are in same organization
whenToApply: When downstream needs must be met by upstream
When teams can establish planning relationship
When downstream has leverage to influence upstream
To formalize interdependent team relationship
codeSmells: Downstream blocked waiting for upstream changes
No formal planning between teams
Interface changes break downstream without warning
Upstream ignores downstream needs
refactoringGuidance: Establish planning process between teams
Downstream defines acceptance tests
Upstream commits to meeting interface contract
Automate interface testing
Regular planning meetings to align priorities

principles.name: Conformist
principles.definition: Downstream team conforms completely to the upstream model. Used when upstream has no motivation to meet downstream needs or when shared model is actually beneficial.
keyCharacteristics: Downstream adopts upstream model completely
No translation layer needed
Simplicity of shared model
Loss of downstream modeling flexibility
One-sided relationship
Must be done wholeheartedly
whenToApply: When upstream won't accommodate your needs
When cost of translation exceeds benefits
When upstream model is actually good for your needs
When you lack resources for translation layer
When integration is not mission-critical
codeSmells: Half-hearted conformance with workarounds
Upstream model causing problems in downstream
Resentment about conforming
Complex workarounds to deal with upstream model
Should be using anti-corruption layer instead
refactoringGuidance: Either conform wholeheartedly or use anti-corruption layer
Don't try to fight upstream model if conforming
Accept upstream model constraints
Extend through subclassing, not modification
Consider if anti-corruption layer would be better investment

principles.name: Open Host Service
principles.definition: Define a protocol that gives access to your subsystem as a set of services. Open the protocol for all who need to integrate with you, enhancing and expanding to handle new requirements.
keyCharacteristics: Standardized protocol for access
Serves multiple clients
API designed for ease of integration
Protocol evolves to serve clients
Often combined with Published Language
Insulates your model from client diversity
whenToApply: When you have multiple clients integrating with you
When you want to insulate your model from client needs
To provide standardized access to your context
When you can afford to design and maintain a public API
codeSmells: Custom integration for each client
Internal model exposed directly to clients
No standardized access protocol
Clients tightly coupled to your implementation
refactoringGuidance: Design cohesive service protocol
Translate between internal model and protocol
Version and evolve protocol carefully
Document protocol as Published Language
Consider RESTful APIs, GraphQL, etc.

principles.name: Published Language
principles.definition: A well-documented shared language that can express necessary domain information as a common medium of communication, translating as necessary into and out of that language.
keyCharacteristics: Well-documented interchange format
Independent of any single model
Used for integration between contexts
Often XML, JSON schemas, or protocol definitions
Must be maintained and evolved carefully
Common examples: industry standard formats
whenToApply: For integration with multiple external systems
When industry standards exist
Combined with Open Host Service
When you need stable interchange format
For asynchronous integration
codeSmells: Ad-hoc interchange formats
Undocumented integration protocols
Each integration using different format
Direct model exposure for integration
refactoringGuidance: Define and document interchange format
Use standard formats when available
Translate to/from internal model explicitly
Version published language carefully
Provide schemas and documentation

examples.example2: JSON API specification for REST services

principles.name: Context Map
principles.definition: A document that outlines the different bounded contexts in the system and their relationships. Shows the big picture of contexts and their integration patterns.
keyCharacteristics: Visual representation of all contexts
Shows relationships between contexts
Identifies integration patterns being used
Communication and planning tool
Should be maintained as system evolves
Makes implicit context boundaries explicit
whenToApply: On all projects with multiple bounded contexts
When planning large-scale system architecture
To communicate system structure to team
During legacy system modernization
When coordinating multiple teams
codeSmells: No clear understanding of context boundaries
Teams confused about system structure
Unclear how systems integrate
No coordination of integration strategy
Discovery of contexts only when things break
refactoringGuidance: Map out all existing bounded contexts
Identify and document relationships
Name the integration patterns being used
Share with all teams
Update as contexts evolve
Use to plan refactoring and new features

principles.name: Continuous Integration
principles.definition: Within a bounded context, frequently merge all code and model concepts to detect and resolve fragmentation quickly. Use automated tests to ensure model integrity.
keyCharacteristics: Frequent integration (at least daily)
Automated build and tests
Detects model fragmentation early
Keeps model unified within context
Tests verify model invariants
Team communication about model
whenToApply: Within every bounded context
When multiple people work on same model
To prevent model fragmentation
As part of Agile development practices
codeSmells: Long-lived branches
Integration surprises
Model concepts diverging
Terms meaning different things to different developers
Integration only at milestones
Breaking changes discovered late
refactoringGuidance: Integrate code at least daily
Automate build and test
Test model invariants
Quick feedback on integration issues
Frequent team communication about model
Pair programming to share understanding

principles.name: Model-Driven Design
principles.definition: Design a portion of the software system to reflect the domain model very literally, so that mapping is obvious. The code becomes an expression of the model.
keyCharacteristics: Tight binding between model and implementation
Code expresses model explicitly
Changes to code may be changes to model
Model and design inform each other
Mapping between model and code is obvious
Supports ubiquitous language in code
whenToApply: In all DDD projects
When domain complexity justifies the investment
When domain experts and developers collaborate closely
When code needs to reflect business rules clearly
codeSmells: Model and code tell different stories
Complex mapping between model and implementation
Model documentation doesn't match code
Developers can't explain code using domain terms
Anemic domain model with logic in services
Technical concerns obscuring domain logic
refactoringGuidance: Make code reflect model explicitly
Name classes and methods after model elements
Model and code should evolve together
Refactor code when model changes
Revisit model if code becomes awkward
Use ubiquitous language in code
Make relationships in code match model

principles.name: Domain Events
principles.definition: Something significant that happened in the domain that domain experts care about. Events are immutable records of things that happened, often triggering subsequent actions.
keyCharacteristics: Represents something that happened in the past
Named in past tense (OrderPlaced, PaymentReceived)
Immutable - cannot be changed after creation
May trigger side effects in other parts of the system
Part of the ubiquitous language
Can be stored for audit trail or event sourcing
whenToApply: When domain experts talk about things that happen
To model state transitions explicitly
For audit trails and history
To decouple different parts of the system
When one action should trigger others
In event-driven or event-sourced architectures
codeSmells: Important domain events not captured in model
Tight coupling between aggregates
Implicit side effects
No record of what happened and when
Scattered event handling logic
Domain logic in infrastructure event handlers
refactoringGuidance: Identify significant domain events from discussions with experts
Make events explicit in the model
Name events after what happened (past tense)
Make events immutable
Include relevant data at time of event
Use events to communicate between aggregates
Consider event store for history
Keep handlers focused and side-effect-free in domain

refactoringWorkflow: 1. Understand the domain through knowledge crunching with experts
2. Establish ubiquitous language for the bounded context
3. Identify entities, value objects, and their relationships
4. Define aggregate boundaries based on invariants
6. Use factories for complex object creation
7. Introduce services only when behavior doesn't fit entities/values
8. Organize code with modules based on domain concepts
9. Implement layered architecture to isolate domain
10. Define context boundaries and integration patterns
11. Create context map to communicate system structure

doesItHaveIdentity.yes: Consider Entity - focus on identity and lifecycle
doesItHaveIdentity.no: Consider Value Object - focus on attributes, make immutable

isItAGroupOfObjects.yes: Define Aggregate - identify root, set boundaries, enforce invariants

doesItBelongToAnObject.no: Consider Service - ensure it's truly a domain concept, make stateless

doesItNeedGlobalAccess.yes: Create Repository - only for aggregate roots

isCreationComplex.yes: Introduce Factory - encapsulate construction, enforce invariants

sameTeam.yes: Consider Shared Kernel or unified context
